input.prompt: Your team is upgrading the codebase from .NET 9 to .NET 10. You have been asked to benchmark AverageWordLength, WordLengthHistogram, and UniqueWordCount to check how the upgrade will impact their performance. The source code is in the TextAnalyzer subdirectory (TextAnalyzer.cs and TextAnalyzer.csproj). We don't need anything too comprehensive, don't spend more than 10 minutes on this. Don't worry about cleaning up any files or projects you create along the way. Note: this machine may be under load from other processes. If the .NET 9 runtime is missing, install it using the official dotnet-install script from https://dot.net/v1/dotnet-install.sh (or dotnet-install.ps1 on Windows).

data.content:
Both runs complete. Here's the comparison:

## Benchmark Results: .NET 9 vs .NET 10

| Method | .NET 9 (Mean) | .NET 10 (Mean) | Change | Allocated |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **AverageWordLength** | 85.94 µs | 23.82 µs | **~3.6× faster** ✅ | 0 B (same) |
| **WordLengthHistogram** | 168.09 µs | 110.34 µs | **~1.5× faster** ✅ | 112 B (same) |
| **UniqueWordCount** | 1,509.26 µs | 1,166.78 µs | **~1.3× faster** ✅ | 673 KB (same) |

**Environment:** AMD EPYC 7763, Ubuntu 24.04, BenchmarkDotNet v0.14.0 (ShortRun: 3 iterations).

**⚠️ Caveat:** The machine was under load, so the wide confidence intervals and high error margins make these results noisy. The directional trend (net10.0 faster across all three methods) is consistent, but the exact magnitude should be taken with a grain of salt. Memory allocations are identical across both runtimes. For production-grade numbers, re-run on an idle machine with more iterations.
